    Home › Types › Propliners & flying boats

    Propliners & flying boats

    The giants of the propeller age — and the turboprops still working today.

    Aircraft here5
    Longest66.65 m
    Combined built1,874
    First flights1947–1998
    What it means

    The category, explained

    The propeller era produced aircraft of a scale that still surprises people. Piston and turboprop giants carried the ambitions of their age: the Bristol Brabazon's ocean-liner luxury, the Hughes H-4's 97.54-metre wingspan, and the Tu-114's 770 km/h — still the fastest a propeller airliner has ever flown.

    The giants are gone, but propellers never left. On short regional routes the turboprop is simply the better answer: the ATR 72 and the Dash 8-400 burn roughly half the fuel of a comparable jet for a journey barely any slower, and they work short, steep runways that jets cannot touch. Over 1,800 of the two are flying today.

    The aircraft

    Every propliners & flying boat here

    Silhouettes are sized by real wingspan — bigger aircraft draw bigger.

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
